verdicts.title: Unauthenticated AJAX Endpoint Exposes Sensitive Data (kirki_get_apis / nopriv)
verdicts.description: The `kirki_get_apis` action is registered for BOTH `wp_ajax_kirki_get_apis` (authenticated) and `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_get_apis` (unauthenticated). The handler in `kirki_get_apis()` does call `HelperFunctions::verify_nonce('wp_rest')` for most endpoints, and then `is_admin()` to block most responses. However, there is a critical bypass path: when the request contains an `Editor-Preview-Token` header, `is_api_call_from_editor_preview()` returns `true`, and the function returns early with `wp_send_json_error('Not authorized')` ONLY if the token is invalid — but if the token IS valid, the nonce check is skipped entirely and execution falls through to `is_admin()`. More critically, the `kirki_post_apis_nopriv` action is registered on `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_post_apis_nopriv` and its handler (`kirki_post_apis_nopriv`) has NO nonce check at all; it relies only on `is_api_call_from_editor_preview()` returning true and the token being valid. The Editor-Preview-Token is a stored option value readable from the DB, and if the read-only access feature is enabled, that token is effectively a credential that can be replayed by any unauthenticated attacker who can obtain it.
verdicts.remediation: Remove the `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_get_apis` registration entirely if public access to GET data is not needed. For `kirki_post_apis_nopriv`, add a proper nonce check or rate-limit the token-based auth path. The editor preview token mechanism should be validated before any data is returned, not just checked for the error path.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via Unvalidated $_GET Parameters in wp_redirect()
verdicts.description: In `TemplateRedirection.php`, the `check_and_redirect_to_custom_utility_page()` method passes the entire `$_GET` superglobal into `add_query_arg()` and then feeds the result directly to `wp_redirect()`. No validation or sanitization is performed on any of the query parameter keys or values. While `wp_redirect()` does not allow completely external URLs (it internally calls `wp_sanitize_redirect()`), an attacker can craft parameters that cause URL scheme injection (e.g., `//evil.com` protocol-relative URLs, or inject headers if the query args are processed specially). Additionally, any existing GET parameters from the login form are blindly forwarded to the custom login page URL, which may expose sensitive query string data such as `redirect_to` values. This fires on the unauthenticated WordPress login form hooks (`login_form_login`, `login_form_register`, etc.).
verdicts.proof_of_concept:
Browse to: https://target.example.com/wp-login.php?action=login&redirect_to=//evil.com%2Ffake-login&sensitive_param=leaked_value
If a custom kirki login utility page is configured, the browser is redirected to: https://target.example.com/custom-login?redirect_to=//evil.com/fake-login&sensitive_param=leaked_value
verdicts.remediation: Replace `$query_args = $_GET` with an explicit allowlist of permitted query parameters (e.g., only `redirect_to`, and validate it with `wp_validate_redirect()`). Use `wp_safe_redirect()` instead of `wp_redirect()` to enforce same-host redirects.

verdicts.title: Sensitive File Download Endpoint Lacks Nonce Verification (CSRF)
verdicts.description: The `download_zip_endpoint()` in `API.php` (triggered on `init` when `$_GET['page-export']` and `$_GET['file-name']` are set) checks `HelperFunctions::has_access(KIRKI_ACCESS_LEVELS['FULL_ACCESS'])` but explicitly has a `TODO: need to check nonce` comment with no nonce verification implemented. An authenticated user with FULL_ACCESS who is tricked into visiting a crafted URL can have a ZIP file from the uploads directory served to a third-party site (CSRF download). The `basename()` call prevents path traversal above the uploads directory, so this is CSRF-only, not a path traversal, but the missing nonce allows cross-site request forgery against any FULL_ACCESS user.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: Embed in an attacker page: — when visited by an authenticated FULL_ACCESS user, the ZIP is served in their browser context.
verdicts.remediation: Add nonce verification before serving the file: generate and embed a nonce in the download link on the admin side, then verify it with `wp_verify_nonce()` in `download_zip_endpoint()`. Also remove the TODO comment.

verdicts.title: Frontend REST API Routes Accessible Without Authentication (Collection, Comments, Users, Terms, Form Submit)
verdicts.vulnerability_class: Broken Access Control / Unauthenticated REST Endpoint
verdicts.description: The `FrontendRESTController` base class defines `get_item_permissions_check()` as simply `return true`, meaning all routes registered under `kirki/v1/frontend/` are publicly accessible without authentication. These include: `/collection` (returns rendered HTML collection blocks), `/comments` (returns rendered comment blocks), `/users` (returns rendered user collection blocks), `/terms` (returns rendered term data), and `/form` (accepts form submissions). The `/form` endpoint in particular triggers file uploads (`media_handle_upload()`), database inserts, webhook calls, and email notifications. An unauthenticated attacker can spam form submissions, trigger email floods, upload files to the media library (if the form has a file field), and cause webhook requests to arbitrary third-party URLs.
verdicts.remediation: Override `get_item_permissions_check()` in `FrontendRESTController` to return `is_user_logged_in()` for user-specific endpoints. For public form submissions, add rate-limiting, CSRF token validation (the `_wpnonce` field is accepted but never verified), and enforce reCAPTCHA on all form endpoints regardless of configuration.

verdicts.title: CSRF on Form Submission REST Endpoint (_wpnonce Accepted but Not Verified)
verdicts.description: The `FormController::save_form_data()` endpoint checks that the `_wpnonce` parameter is present (`isset($wpnonce)`) but never actually calls `wp_verify_nonce()` or `check_ajax_referer()` against it. The code only validates that the field exists. Combined with the fact that the endpoint is publicly accessible (VULN-004), this means any cross-origin request can trigger form submissions. reCAPTCHA verification is skipped entirely when `g-recaptcha-token` is not in the POST data, so a direct API call bypasses bot protection. An attacker controlling a page visited by a victim can silently submit forms on the victim's behalf, including forms that trigger webhook exfiltration of form data.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: The _wpnonce check on line 108 is: `if (!isset($form_meta_data_base64) || !isset($wpnonce))` — this verifies the parameter exists, but the value is never passed to wp_verify_nonce(). Send any string as _wpnonce and the check passes.
verdicts.remediation: Replace the `isset($wpnonce)` check with `wp_verify_nonce($wpnonce, 'kirki_form_nonce_' . $form_id)`. Generate and embed a matching nonce when rendering the form on the frontend via the Preview PHP renderer.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated AJAX kirki_get_apis Exposes Collaboration Actions Without Auth
verdicts.description: In `kirki_get_apis()`, the `collect-collaboration-actions` and `delete-collaboration-connection` endpoints are explicitly excluded from the `verify_nonce('wp_rest')` call (there is a TODO comment: 'Need to verify for collaboration'). Instead they call `user_can_access_wp_apis()` which requires `is_user_logged_in()`. However, the entire method is registered on `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_get_apis`, meaning unauthenticated requests reach this method. The subsequent `is_admin()` check at line 329 does block most endpoints for non-admin contexts, but the collaboration endpoints only check `user_can_access_wp_apis()` and then reach their handlers before the `is_admin()` guard. For `collect-collaboration-actions` in particular, `Collaboration::send_actions()` may return buffered data depending on the session ID.
verdicts.remediation: Remove `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_get_apis` registration. For any genuinely public GET APIs, create a separate dedicated nopriv handler with tight controls. Add nonce verification to the collaboration endpoints.

verdicts.title: Unvalidated File Name in ZIP Download (Reflected Content-Disposition Header Injection Risk)
verdicts.description: In `API::downloadZIP()`, `$_GET['file-name']` is passed through `HelperFunctions::sanitize_text()` and `basename()`, then embedded directly into the `Content-Disposition` header value: `header('Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="' . $file_name . '"')`. The `sanitize_text_field()` strips tags and extra whitespace but does not strip all characters that could be meaningful in an HTTP header context. If a file name contains a double-quote character, it could break out of the filename attribute. However, `basename()` limits the effective path to a single filename component, which greatly reduces the attack surface. The check that the extension must be `.zip` further limits exploitability. Risk is low but present for filename values like `foo".zip`.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: Requires FULL_ACCESS. Request: ?page-export=true&file-name=test%22.zip — results in header: Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="test".zip" which is malformed.
verdicts.remediation: Escape the filename for use in the Content-Disposition header using `addslashes()` or RFC 5987 encoding: `filename*=UTF-8''` followed by rawurlencode of the sanitized name. Also verify the file actually exists in the uploads directory before sending headers.

verdicts.title: Object Injection via unserialize() on Database-Retrieved Form Data
verdicts.description: In `Ajax/Form.php` lines 809-810, data retrieved from the form submissions table is unserialized using `@unserialize()` without checking if the value is a known safe type. The data was originally serialized by `FormController::insert_form_data()` before being stored. An attacker who can directly write to the wp_kirki_form_data table (e.g., a compromised admin, or via a separate SQL injection vulnerability) could plant a serialized PHP object. Upon display in the admin form viewer, this would trigger any POP chain available in the codebase. Under normal conditions (no separate SQLi), this requires admin-level DB access, making it low exploitability. The `maybe_unserialize()` calls in `ExportImport/TemplateExport.php` follow the same pattern but operate on `get_post_meta()` data which requires prior write access to the DB.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: Requires direct DB write access. INSERT into wp_kirki_form_data a row with input_value containing a serialized PHP object with a __wakeup or __destruct POP chain. Trigger by loading the form data admin view.
verdicts.remediation: Replace `@unserialize()` with `json_encode/json_decode` for storing complex values, or use `maybe_unserialize()` with an `allowed_classes: false` parameter (PHP 7.0+) to prevent object instantiation: `unserialize($value, ['allowed_classes' => false])`.

verdicts.title: Session ID Taken from Attacker-Controlled Cookie (Session Fixation)
verdicts.description: The `HelperFunctions::get_session_id()` method reads the session ID directly from `$_COOKIE['kirki_session_id']` without any server-side validation that the value was issued by the server. An attacker who knows any valid `kirki_session_id` value (e.g., by observing another user's cookie, or by having previously submitted a form themselves) can fixate their session ID to match a victim's. The form submission flow uses `session_id()` (PHP native session, different from kirki_session_id) for the entry-limit check, but `kirki_session_id` is used to look up `form_config` from a transient. A malicious actor could craft a `kirki_session_id` cookie value to collide with another user's transient key and potentially retrieve their cached form configuration.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: Set cookie kirki_session_id=<known_victim_session_uuid> and POST to the form endpoint to retrieve or interfere with the victim's session transient data.
verdicts.remediation: Validate that the session cookie value matches a server-generated HMAC or signature before trusting it. Alternatively, use WordPress's native transient system with user-ID scoping rather than session cookies.

verdicts.title: RCE Flags (array_map) - False Positives
verdicts.description: The static analysis tool flagged multiple `array_map` calls as potential RCE vectors. Manual review confirms these are false positives. The flagged `array_map` calls in `DynamicContent.php` use arrow functions (`fn($key, $value) => ...`) operating on WordPress-internal data (`wp_roles()->role_names`, `get_users()`). None of them pass user-controlled callbacks or use variable function names. No actual shell execution (`shell_exec`, `exec`, `passthru`, `system`, `popen`, `proc_open`) was found that takes user-controlled input.
verdicts.remediation: No action needed. Consider tuning the static analysis rule to exclude PHP arrow function syntax in array_map calls.

summary:
Kirki (priority score 516) is a full-featured page builder plugin with a large attack surface. Six confirmed or likely real vulnerabilities were identified:

1. **Highest Risk**: The frontend REST API routes (`kirki/v1/frontend/*`) are publicly accessible with no authentication at all (`return true` in permission_callback). The form submission endpoint in particular allows unauthenticated file uploads, spam emails to admin, and webhook calls to arbitrary URLs. The `_wpnonce` field is accepted but never cryptographically verified.

2. **High Risk**: The `wp_ajax_nopriv_kirki_get_apis` registration exposes the main AJAX dispatcher to unauthenticated callers, bypassing the nonce check for collaboration endpoints and enabling the editor-preview-token pathway without a nonce.

3. **Medium Risk**: The `wp_redirect()` call in `TemplateRedirection.php` passes the raw `$_GET` superglobal into `add_query_arg()` without allowlisting parameters, enabling phishing-quality open redirects from the login page.

4. **Medium Risk**: The ZIP file download endpoint (`?page-export=true&file-name=*`) checks authorization but has an explicit TODO for missing nonce verification, enabling CSRF against authenticated FULL_ACCESS users.

5. **Low Risk**: PHP `unserialize()` on database-retrieved form data is unsafe if a DB write path exists, and the session cookie is trusted without HMAC validation (session fixation risk).

The RCE flags from static analysis are confirmed false positives (array_map with arrow functions). The `maybe_unserialize()` calls on `get_option()`/`get_post_meta()` data are medium-low risk since they require prior DB write access.
